stem: A cancer detection model has 99% accuracy on a dataset where 99% of patients are healthy. This model is likely:

options.text: Useless — it probably predicts 'healthy' for everyone and misses all cancer cases

explanation_after: On a dataset that's 99% healthy, a model that always predicts 'healthy' gets 99% accuracy without detecting a single cancer. Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets — use precision/recall instead.
Recall = TP / (TP + FN). It measures 'of all actual positives, how many did I correctly identify?' High recall means few positives are missed.

stem: For a medical diagnosis model, why is recall (sensitivity) usually more important than precision?

options.text: Because medical models are trained on large datasets
Because missing a positive case (false negative) is more dangerous than a false alarm
Because precision is harder to compute
Because medical datasets are always balanced

explanation_after: A false negative (telling a sick patient they're healthy) can be life-threatening. A false positive (unnecessary follow-up test) is inconvenient but manageable. High recall ensures sick patients aren't missed.

stem: What does a ROC AUC of 0.5 mean?

options.text: The model performs no better than random guessing

explanation_after: AUC = 0.5 means the model performs equivalently to random guessing. AUC = 1.0 is perfect. AUC < 0.5 means the model is worse than random (often indicating inverted labels).

stem: 5-fold cross-validation provides more reliable evaluation than a single train/test split because:

options.text: Each example appears in the test fold exactly once, giving a less variance-dependent estimate
It removes the need for a test set

explanation_after: With 5-fold CV, every example is tested exactly once across 5 different train/test splits. The average metric is more stable than a single split, which might be unusually easy or hard by chance.

stem: F1 score is used instead of accuracy when:

options.text: You want a single metric that balances precision and recall, especially for imbalanced classes
You have more than two classes
Your model is a neural network

explanation_after: F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It's preferred when classes are imbalanced or when both false positives and false negatives matter — where accuracy can be misleading.
